section.title: The Demoniac, Jairus, and the Bleeding Woman
section.sectionRationale: Three unforgettable rescues show Jesus' authority reaching to the furthest extremities of human ruin. He frees a man enslaved by a legion of demons among the tombs, heals a woman drained by twelve years of bleeding, and raises a synagogue leader's dead daughter. Demons, chronic defilement, and death itself all yield to His word and His touch.
section.thesis: Jesus is Lord over the demonic, the defiling, and the dead, meeting the most hopeless cases, the raging outcast, the unclean and impoverished woman, and a lifeless child, with a power and mercy that restore them whole, and He calls for faith rather than fear.
section.frameNote: Three rescues, with the last two interwoven: the Gerasene demoniac set free; the bleeding woman healed by touching Jesus' cloak; and the raising of Jairus's daughter.

units.label: The Gerasene demoniac set free
units.note: Legion cast out, the man restored

verses.text: On the other side of the sea, they arrived in the region of the Gerasenes.
As soon as Jesus got out of the boat, He was met by a man with an unclean spirit, who was coming from the tombs.
This man had been living in the tombs and could no longer be restrained, even with chains.
Though he was often bound with chains and shackles, he had broken the chains and shattered the shackles. Now there was no one with the strength to subdue him.
Night and day in the tombs and in the mountains he kept crying out and cutting himself with stones.
When the man saw Jesus from a distance, he ran and fell on his knees before Him.
And he shouted in a loud voice, “What do You want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg You before God not to torture me!”
For Jesus had already declared, “Come out of this man, you unclean spirit!”
“What is your name?” Jesus asked. “My name is {{legion|Legion}},” he replied, “for we are many.”
And he begged Jesus repeatedly not to send them out of that region.
There on the nearby hillside a large herd of pigs was feeding.
So the demons begged Jesus, “Send us to the pigs, so that we may enter them.”
He gave them permission, and the unclean spirits came out and went into the pigs, and the herd of about two thousand rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned in the water.
Those tending the pigs ran off and reported this in the town and countryside, and the people went out to see what had happened.
When they came to Jesus, they saw the man who had been possessed by the legion of demons sitting there, clothed and in his right mind; and they were afraid.
Those who had seen it described what had happened to the demon-possessed man and also to the pigs.
And the people began to beg Jesus to leave their region.
As He was getting into the boat, the man who had been possessed by the demons begged to go with Him.
But Jesus would not allow him. “Go home to your own people,” He said, “and tell them how much the Lord has done for you, and what mercy He has shown you.”
So the man went away and began to proclaim throughout the Decapolis how much Jesus had done for him. And everyone was amazed.

units.label: The bleeding woman healed
units.note: if only I touch His garments

verses.text: When Jesus had again crossed by boat to the other side, a large crowd gathered around Him beside the sea.
A synagogue leader named Jairus arrived, and seeing Jesus, he fell at His feet
and pleaded with Him urgently, “My little daughter is near death. Please come and place Your hands on her, so that she will be healed and live.”
So Jesus went with him, and a large crowd followed and pressed around Him.
And a woman was there who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years.
She had borne much agony under the care of many physicians and had spent all she had, but to no avail. Instead, her condition had only grown worse.
When the woman heard about Jesus, she came up through the crowd behind Him and touched His cloak.
For she kept saying, “If only I touch His garments, I will be {{sozo|healed}}.”
Immediately her bleeding stopped, and she sensed in her body that she was healed of her affliction.
At once Jesus was aware that power had gone out from Him. Turning to the crowd, He asked, “Who touched My garments?”
His disciples answered, “You can see the crowd pressing in on You, and yet You ask, ‘Who touched Me?’”
But He kept looking around to see who had done this.
Then the woman, knowing what had happened to her, came and fell down before Him trembling in fear, and she told Him the whole truth.
“Daughter,” said Jesus, “your {{pistis|faith}} has healed you. Go in peace and be free of your affliction.”

units.label: The raising of Jairus's daughter
units.note: little girl, I say to you, get up

verses.text: While He was still speaking, messengers from the house of Jairus arrived and said, “Your daughter is dead; why bother the Teacher anymore?”
But Jesus overheard their conversation and said to Jairus, “Do not be afraid; just {{pisteuo|believe}}.”
And He did not allow anyone to accompany Him except Peter, James, and John the brother of James.
When they arrived at the house of the synagogue leader, Jesus saw the commotion and the people weeping and wailing loudly.
He went inside and asked, “Why all this commotion and weeping? The child is not dead, but asleep.”
And they laughed at Him. After He had put them all outside, He took the child’s father and mother and His own companions, and went in to see the child.
Taking her by the hand, Jesus said, “{{talitha|Talitha koum}}!” which means, “Little girl, I say to you, get up!”
Immediately the girl got up and began to walk around (she was twelve years old). And at once they were utterly astounded.
Then Jesus gave strict orders that no one should know about this, and He told them to give her something to eat.

legion.gloss: Legion, a Latin loanword for a Roman army division of up to six thousand soldiers. The demons name themselves Legion because they are many; the military term conveys the sheer force enslaving the man, and its utter powerlessness before Jesus, who dismisses the whole host with a word.

sozo.gloss: To save, rescue, heal, make whole. The same verb covers bodily healing and eternal salvation. The woman believes touching Jesus' garments will make her well, and Jesus tells her that her faith has saved her, so her healing becomes a picture of the fuller rescue faith receives.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust. It was not the magic of the cloak but the woman's faith that laid hold of Jesus' power; He says her faith has saved her. Faith reaches out to Christ in the crowd of many who merely press against Him, and it receives what He freely gives.

pisteuo.gloss: To believe, trust. As the news of death arrives, Jesus tells Jairus only do not be afraid, just believe. Faith is set against fear; Jairus is called to keep trusting Jesus even past the point where death seems to end all hope.

talitha.gloss: Little girl, get up, the Aramaic words Jesus actually spoke, preserved by Mark and then translated for his readers. The tender, homely command shows Jesus speaking to the dead child as gently as a parent waking a sleeper, and death obeys.

basic.context: Mark shows Jesus overpowering three enemies. First, across the lake, a man tormented by a legion of demons lives naked among the tombs, uncontrollable, until Jesus casts the demons out into a herd of pigs and the man is found clothed and sane. The frightened locals ask Jesus to leave, but the freed man goes to tell everyone what the Lord has done. Back across the water, a synagogue leader named Jairus begs Jesus to heal his dying daughter. On the way, a woman who has bled for twelve years touches Jesus' cloak and is instantly healed; Jesus stops to honour her faith. Then news comes that the girl has died, but Jesus goes on, takes her by the hand, and raises her with a word.
basic.hermeneutics: These are demonstrations of who Jesus is: Lord over demons, disease, and death. Notice the recurring themes of faith versus fear and of Jesus touching the unclean, the demoniac, the bleeding woman, the corpse, without being defiled; instead He makes them whole. The interwoven stories of Jairus and the woman invite comparison: both involve twelve years, both need desperate faith.
basic.originalLanguages: The demons call themselves legion, an army, yet flee at a word. The woman is sure that touching Jesus will make her sozo, saved or healed, and Jesus credits her pistis, faith. To Jairus He says simply pisteuo, believe. And He raises the girl with the Aramaic talitha koum, little girl, get up.
basic.typology: Jesus touching the unclean and the dead without contracting defilement reverses the flow of the law: holiness overcomes uncleanness. His raising of the girl is a foretaste of His own resurrection and of the general resurrection He guarantees.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son of the Most High God, as the demon confesses. He commands demons, heals the incurable, and raises the dead. Every scene answers Mark's question, who is this, with the same reply: the Lord of life.
basic.god: God's power and mercy meet the most hopeless cases. The Lord has done great things and shown mercy, as Jesus tells the freed man to report. God calls for faith in the face of fear and even death.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 65 pictures those who dwell among tombs. Leviticus 15 gives the law of the bleeding woman's uncleanness. John 11 records the raising of Lazarus, a fuller sign of the same power.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom that comes hidden as seed also comes with power to reclaim what evil, sickness, and death have stolen. Jesus frees the enslaved, cleanses the defiled, and raises the dead, previews of the final rescue when the King will banish every demon, wipe away every tear, and raise His people to life that never ends.

context.literary: These three miracles form the climax of Mark's opening display of Jesus' authority, following the stilling of the storm. Mark famously interleaves the woman's healing inside the Jairus story, a sandwich that lets each illuminate the other: both feature twelve years, desperate need, and the movement from fear to faith. The demoniac scene, set in Gentile territory, extends Jesus' authority beyond Israel.
context.historical: The Gerasene region was Gentile, marked by tombs and pig herds, both sources of ceremonial uncleanness for Jews. A woman with a discharge of blood was perpetually unclean under Leviticus 15, cut off from worship and normal contact, and her touch would ordinarily transmit that uncleanness. Professional mourners and flute players explain the loud wailing at Jairus's house.
context.canonical: The demoniac dwelling among tombs and mountains recalls the desolation of Isaiah 65:4. Jesus overcoming uncleanness by His touch fulfils the promise of a holiness stronger than defilement. The raising of the girl anticipates John 11 and 1 Corinthians 15, where Christ is the resurrection and the firstfruits of those who sleep.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate that Jesus' authority reaches the furthest extremities of human ruin, the demonic, the chronically unclean, and the dead, and to contrast faith with fear, so that the reader trusts the Lord of life rather than shrinking back like the frightened crowd.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe Jesus' unique power; they are not a formula guaranteeing that every sick or bereaved believer will be healed or raised now. What is prescriptive is the call to faith over fear and the assurance that Jesus is Lord over every power that harms us, a hope consummated at the resurrection.

debates.question: Why did Jesus allow the demons to enter the pigs, destroying the herd?

views.label: A visible proof of real deliverance and the demons' destructiveness
views.case: The drowning herd shows unmistakably that the many demons truly left the man and reveals their nature: they destroy whatever they possess, making plain the ruin from which the man was saved.

views.label: The value of one restored person over property
views.case: Jesus permits a loss of unclean animals to secure the rescue of a human being made in God's image; the townsfolk's dismay at their pigs exposes hearts that valued property over a restored man.

debates.bearing: Both underline that a single soul reclaimed from the powers of darkness is worth more than any herd, and that the deliverance was real and complete.

debates.question: Why does Jesus stop to find the woman who was already healed?

views.label: To turn a secret touch into open faith
views.case: Jesus will not let her slip away with a magical notion of the cloak; He draws her out to confess the whole truth so her faith is named and honoured and she leaves in peace, called daughter.

views.label: To teach the crowd and Jairus about faith
views.case: By publicly commending her faith, Jesus strengthens the watching Jairus, whose own daughter is dying, showing that faith, not fear, is the way, just before the news of death arrives.

debates.bearing: Both readings show Jesus caring for the person, not merely the cure, and lifting a trembling, hidden faith into open assurance and peace.

study.originalLanguages: The demons name themselves legion, a Roman army division of thousands, boasting of their number even as they flee at Jesus' single command, a picture of overwhelming evil that is nothing before Him. The bleeding woman is convinced that touching Jesus will make her sozo, a verb that means both to heal and to save, and Jesus credits not the cloak but her pistis, her faith, saying it has saved her. To Jairus, staggered by the news of death, He says only do not fear, just pisteuo, believe. Then, taking the dead child's hand, He speaks the homely Aramaic talitha koum, little girl, get up, and death releases her at His word.

typology.fulfillment: Christ touching the demoniac, the unclean woman, and the corpse without being defiled, cleansing rather than being contaminated, the true and greater priest, Hebrews 7:26 and Leviticus 15:25-27.

typology.type: The raising of the dead child
typology.fulfillment: Christ the resurrection and the life, whose word over the girl foreshadows His own rising and the resurrection of all His people, John 11:25 and 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: The freed captive sent to proclaim
typology.fulfillment: The delivered sinner commissioned to tell of God's mercy, a first Gentile witness pointing to the worldwide mission, Mark 5:19-20 and Psalm 107:2.

study.christ: Jesus is confessed by the demon as Son of the Most High God, and the chapter proves the title true. He speaks and a legion of demons flees. He is touched by an unclean woman and, far from being defiled, heals her and calls her daughter. He takes a dead girl by the hand, and life returns at His tender word. Where the law said uncleanness spreads by contact, in Jesus it is holiness and life that spread by His touch. He is the Lord over the three great tyrants of the human condition, the devil, disease, and death, and His mastery here is a preview of the cross and empty tomb where He defeats them all for good.
study.god: The God revealed in Jesus is mighty to save and rich in mercy. He does not keep His distance from the ruined and defiled but comes to the tombs, into the crowd, and inside the house of death, to make people whole. He values one restored human being above a whole herd of property. And He meets terror with the steady call to trust Him: do not be afraid, just believe. The great things done here are, as Jesus says, the Lord's doing and the Lord's mercy, the character of God shining through the Son.

crossReferences.note: The law making a woman with a discharge of blood unclean, and all she touches unclean, the barrier Jesus reverses by healing her.
Those who sit among tombs and unclean places, the picture of the demoniac's desolation before Jesus restores him.
I am the resurrection and the life, the truth enacted when Jesus raises Jairus's daughter.
Christ the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, guaranteeing the resurrection this raising foreshadows.

study.oneStory: The kingdom that comes hidden as a seed also comes with power to reclaim everything evil, sickness, and death have seized. In one sweep Jesus frees a man enslaved by a legion of demons, heals a woman that twelve years and every physician could not, and raises a dead child with a word. Each rescue is a signpost to the great rescue to come, when the King who touched the unclean and called the dead to rise will banish every demon, heal every wound, and raise His people to unending life. Faith, not fear, is the hand that receives it.

quiz.q: What does the deliverance of the Gerasene demoniac reveal about Jesus?

options.text: That He is Lord over even a legion of demons, freeing the most hopelessly enslaved with a word.
options.note: The many demons flee at His command and the man is restored, clothed and sane, Mark 5:8-15.

options.text: That He cares about people tormented by evil.
options.note: True, but the scene's main revelation is His sovereign authority over the whole host of demons.

options.text: That demons are stronger than most people realize.
options.note: The demons are strong, but the point is their utter powerlessness before Jesus, not their strength.

options.text: That Jesus needed the pigs to defeat the demons.
options.note: Jesus commands the demons directly; the pigs only display that the deliverance was real.

quiz.q: What actually healed the woman who touched Jesus' cloak?

options.text: Her faith laying hold of Jesus, who says her faith has healed her.
options.note: Mark 5:34; Jesus credits her faith, not the fabric, distinguishing her from the crowd merely pressing in.

options.text: Touching the special cloth of Jesus' garment.
options.note: She did touch His cloak, but Jesus makes clear it was her faith in Him, not magic in the cloth, that healed her.

options.text: The sheer press of the crowd around Jesus.
options.note: Many pressed against Him; only her believing touch drew healing power.

options.text: The skill of the many physicians she had seen.
options.note: The physicians had failed for twelve years; her healing came through faith in Jesus.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say to Jairus when news comes that his daughter has died?

options.text: Do not be afraid; just believe.
options.note: Mark 5:36 sets faith against fear even in the face of death.

options.text: It is too late now; nothing more can be done.
options.note: The opposite of Jesus' words; He calls Jairus to keep believing.

options.text: Your faith alone will raise her without My help.
options.note: It is Jesus who raises her; Jairus is called to trust Him, not to trust his own faith as a power.

options.text: Bring her to the synagogue for prayer.
options.note: Jesus does go to the house and act, but His actual word to Jairus is do not fear, just believe.

quiz.q: What is striking about Jesus touching the demoniac, the bleeding woman, and the dead girl?

options.text: He contacts what the law counted unclean, yet is not defiled; instead His holiness and life make them whole.
options.note: Jesus reverses the flow of defilement, cleansing and raising rather than being contaminated.

options.text: He was willing to help people others avoided.
options.note: True, but it misses the deeper point that His touch overcomes uncleanness itself.

options.text: He broke the law of Moses by touching them.
options.note: Rather than being defiled, Jesus overcomes the uncleanness; He is not shown as a lawbreaker but as the greater holiness.

options.text: It shows uncleanness was never a real concern in Israel.
options.note: Uncleanness was a serious barrier; the wonder is that Jesus reverses it.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus tell the freed demoniac to go home and tell what the Lord had done?

options.text: To send him as a witness to God's mercy among his own people in the Decapolis.
options.note: Mark 5:19-20; the restored man proclaims Jesus throughout the Gentile Decapolis.

options.text: Because the man wanted to stay with Jesus but was told to serve at home.
options.note: True that the man wished to follow, but the point is his commission to bear witness to God's mercy.

options.text: To keep the miracle completely secret.
options.note: Here Jesus sends him to tell, not to hide it, unlike the secrecy He often urged in Jewish areas.

options.text: Because Jesus did not want the man near Him.
options.note: Jesus acted in love, giving him a mission, not rejecting him.

questions: The demoniac was beyond anyone's power to help, yet not beyond Jesus. What situation feels beyond hope to you, and what would it mean to bring it to Him?
The woman reached out in faith amid a crowd that only pressed against Jesus. What is the difference between crowding near Jesus and truly touching Him by faith?
Jesus stopped to know and honour the trembling woman personally. How does it comfort you that Jesus wants not just to fix you but to know you?
To Jairus, Jesus said, do not be afraid, just believe. Where is fear crowding out faith in your life, even to the point where a situation feels as final as death?
The freed man was sent to tell his own people how much the Lord had done for him. What has the Lord done for you that your family and neighbours most need to hear?

translationNotes.note: The region is variously named Gerasenes, Gadarenes, or Gergesenes in the manuscripts, referring to the same Gentile district on the eastern shore of the lake; the difference does not affect the account.

Your faith has healed you translates a verb that also means has saved you, tying the bodily healing to the salvation faith receives; Jesus adds the warm address, Daughter, and the blessing, go in peace.

Talitha koum is Aramaic, the everyday language Jesus spoke; Mark preserves the actual words and then translates them, little girl, get up, showing the tender authority with which He addresses the dead child.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 5:1-43. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. The Aramaic Talitha koum is retained as in the BSB and glossed in the lexicon. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
